era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt is in the late Fourth Dynasty or early Fifth Dynasty transition (c. 2686–2181 BCE). In Mesopotamia, the Early Dynastic Period III continues, with competing city-states and the rise of Akkadian influence. The Indus Valley Civilization remains in its mature urban phase. In China, the Xia Dynasty is either legendary or in its early stages; no contemporary written records exist.

events.title: Late Fourth Dynasty Egypt / Early Fifth Dynasty transition
events.description: The transition between Egypt's Fourth and Fifth Dynasties occurs around this time (exact year uncertain). The Fourth Dynasty, marked by the construction of the Great Pyramids, gives way to the Fifth Dynasty, which emphasizes solar religion and administrative decentralization. This shift represents a significant change in royal ideology, with increased prominence given to the priesthood of Ra and a move away from the monumental pyramid-building projects of the Fourth Dynasty.
key_figures: Shepseskaf (last Fourth Dynasty pharaoh, if reign extends to this year)
Userkaf (first Fifth Dynasty pharaoh, if reign begins around this year)
events.certainty_note: The exact year of the Fourth-Fifth Dynasty transition is uncertain; estimates range from c. 2494–2480 BCE depending on chronological system used. This year is assigned by convention within the transition period.

events.title: Continued urban development in Indus Valley Civilization
events.region: Indus Valley (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues its mature phase with active trade, urban administration, and cultural continuity. Archaeological evidence shows no major disruptions or changes in this year or the surrounding decades. The civilization maintains its characteristic features: planned cities, standardized weights and measures, undeciphered script, and extensive trade networks.

sources.name: Archaeological stratigraphy from Indus Valley sites
Radiocarbon dating of organic materials from occupation layers

events.certainty_note: The Indus Valley Civilization's continued existence in this period is archaeologically confirmed, though individual years cannot be precisely distinguished from the archaeological record.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Fourth-Fifth Dynasty transition is contested among Egyptologists. Some scholars place it earlier (c. 2510 BCE), others later (c. 2480 BCE). Radiocarbon evidence has not definitively resolved this dispute. The significance attributed to the 'transition' may be partly historiographic—later sources emphasize the shift in religious ideology, but the actual administrative and political changes may have been more gradual.
historiographic_note: For 2476 BCE, the historical record is dominated by Egyptian and Indus Valley sources. Mesopotamian records from this period are fragmentary and difficult to date precisely. No contemporary written records exist for China, Europe, Africa south of Egypt, or the Americas. The year-by-year precision implied by this research is largely illusory for ancient periods; the archaeological record typically allows dating only to within ±50–100 years.

graph_edges.note: The Fourth-Fifth Dynasty transition overlaps with Userkaf's reign
